name: Sierra de Alcarama – Cornago Loop
name_original: Sierra de San Cristóbal y Alcarama — circular por Cornago y Magaña — Reserva de la Biosfera: Valles del Leza, Jubera, Cidacos, Alhama y Linares
description: This difficult 76.9-mile (123.7 km) road cycling route through the Valles del Leza Biosphere Reserve offers varied terrain and scenic views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique traverse a landscape characterized by the highlands of Soria and the southern valleys of the Iberian System. The terrain features rolling hills and elevated sections, with routes often winding through rural areas and offering views of the surrounding sierras. This region provides varied gradients suitable for road cycling, from long ascents to descents through valleys. The area's physical geography includes a mix of open country and forested sections, typical of inland Spanish mountain ranges. Best road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique The most popular road cycling route is Soria Highlands Loop, a 53.1 miles (85.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 14 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation changes through the Soria highlands. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Southern valleys of the Iberian System in La Rioja, a difficult 81.7 miles (131.5 km) path. This route explores the valleys and elevated sections of the Iberian System, offering a challenging ride. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra de Alcarama – Cornago Loop, a 76.9 miles (123.7 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Alcarama, often completed in about 6 hours 12 minutes. Road cycling around San Pedro Manrique is defined by highland plateaus, deep valleys, and the varied terrain of the Iberian System. The network offers options for different ability levels, though many routes feature significant climbs. The routes in San Pedro Manrique are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 64 road cyclists have used komoot to explore San Pedro Manrique's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: Are there easy road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique?
A: The road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique are generally considered challenging, with many featuring significant climbs and elevation changes. While there are no routes specifically categorized as 'easy' in our selection, some of the shorter routes might be more manageable for experienced riders looking for less extreme distances.

Q: What are the typical distances for road cycling routes in this area?
A: Road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique vary significantly in length. You can find routes ranging from approximately 57 km, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383511516/navajun-from-valdemadera-circular-by-the-views-to-castillo-de-magana-and-cigudosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navajun from Valdemadera&lt;/a&gt;, up to over 130 km, such as the demanding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990763381/southern-valleys-of-the-iberian-system-in-la-rioja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Southern valleys of the Iberian System in La Rioja&lt;/a&gt;. Most routes fall within the 60-130 km range.

Q: Where can I park when road cycling in San Pedro Manrique?
A: Parking is generally available in the villages and towns that serve as starting points for many routes, including San Pedro Manrique itself. Look for designated parking areas or street parking in these rural communities. Always ensure you park responsibly and do not obstruct local access.

Q: Is San Pedro Manrique suitable for beginner road cyclists?
A: Given the terrain characterized by rolling hills, elevated sections, and significant climbs through the Soria highlands and Iberian System, San Pedro Manrique is more suited for intermediate to advanced road cyclists. Beginners might find the routes quite challenging due to the sustained ascents and longer distances.

Q: What kind of challenges can advanced road cyclists expect?
A: Advanced road cyclists will find plenty of challenges, including long ascents and descents, significant elevation gains (often over 1000m per route), and extended distances. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990763381/southern-valleys-of-the-iberian-system-in-la-rioja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Southern valleys of the Iberian System in La Rioja&lt;/a&gt;, with over 2200m of elevation gain, offer a truly demanding experience.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in San Pedro Manrique?
A: Due to the challenging nature of the terrain, with significant climbs and longer distances, most road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique are not typically considered family-friendly for young children or novice riders. Families might prefer exploring flatter, shorter sections of local roads or dedicated cycling paths if available in nearby towns.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in San Pedro Manrique?
A: The best time for road biking in San Pedro Manrique is generally during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, temperatures are milder, and the weather is usually more stable, making for more comfortable riding conditions. Summers can be hot, and winters can bring cold temperatures and snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around San Pedro Manrique are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990927883/soria-highlands-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soria Highlands Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1078451348/sierra-de-alcarama-cornago-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Alcarama – Cornago Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which are designed to bring you back to your starting point.

Q: What can I see along the road cycling routes in San Pedro Manrique?
A: The routes offer views of the Soria highlands and the Iberian System, passing through rural areas and forested sections. You can also explore historical sites and settlements. For example, the region features attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2718792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yanguas Historic Village&lt;/a&gt; and various castles, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2718376" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of la Nava del Marqués de Magaña&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cafes or pubs near the road cycling trails?
A: As the routes often traverse rural areas and smaller villages, you'll find opportunities for refreshments in the main towns and some of the larger villages along the way. It's advisable to plan your stops in advance, especially on longer routes, as services can be sparse between settlements.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in San Pedro Manrique?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the challenging terrain, the stunning views of the Soria highlands, and the sense of accomplishment from tackling the significant climbs. The quiet, rural roads are also frequently highlighted as a major draw.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around San Pedro Manrique?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around San Pedro Manrique, offering a variety of distances and challenges through the region's diverse landscape of highland plateaus and deep valleys.
tips: Located in a fantastic setting, today crossed by a road, this beautiful Gothic church, from the 16th century, stands out for the altarpieces it contains. The location of the church is at the entrance of the valley, after the Sierra de San Cristóbal.
Mountain pass that goes in the direction of Cornago and is just over 9.5 km long. This climb has an average of just under 4.5% on average and is assumed to be nearly 400 meters.

Spectacular town located on the northern side of the Sierra de Alcarama. From a distance you can see how the center is located at the top of a hill with its church as a flag. The group of houses form a very beautiful perspective.
The road that connects the towns of San Felices with Aguilar del Río Alhama takes you along a narrow road that winds before descending to the second town mentioned.

Before reaching the town of Magaña, in one of the curves that descends to the Río Alhama, you can see the Castillo de Magaña and the whole of its town located at one of the meanders of the same river.

faqs.question: What is the estimated duration for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: This road cycling route is approximately 123.7 km long and typically takes around 6 hours and 12 minutes to complete. This duration can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is this a circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a circular road cycling route that takes you through the Sierra de San Cristóbal y Alcarama, passing by Cornago and Magaña.

faqs.question: Where can I park and start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While a specific trailhead isn't designated, you can typically find parking in or near the towns along the route, such as Cornago or Magaña, to begin your circular journey. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is mountainous and varied, characterized by significant climbs and descents. You'll encounter the orography shaped by the Leza, Jubera, Cidacos, and Alhama rivers, with potential for over 2,000 meters of elevation gain. Roads in similar Spanish mountain regions can range from well-paved to more variable quality.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect diverse and captivating natural beauty, including Mediterranean forests, ash groves along rivers, and dramatic crags and canyons. The route offers glimpses of traditional architecture in towns like Cornago and Magaña, and the region is known for its paleontological significance, with dinosaur footprints.

faqs.question: What notable sights will I see on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass several interesting points, including the Church of Santa María, the challenging Puerto de Vallaroso, and the charming town of Cornago. You'll also encounter the Road leading to San Felices and enjoy Views of the Castillo de Magaña.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult. Given the mountainous terrain of the Biosphere Reserve and the significant elevation changes (over 2,000 meters of ascent), it requires good to very good fitness and experience with challenging road cycling.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route would generally be during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder conditions and potential for snow at higher elevations, making cycling more challenging.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for this Biosphere Reserve?
faqs.answer: No, you do not typically need specific permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle through the Reserva de la Biosfera: Valles del Leza, Jubera, Cidacos, Alhama y Linares. However, always respect local regulations and conservation efforts within the reserve.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a road cycling path, and dogs are generally not suitable companions for such long and difficult rides, if you are stopping in towns or natural areas, it's important to keep dogs on a leash and adhere to local regulations within the Biosphere Reserve to protect wildlife.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through towns like Cornago and Magaña, you should be able to find cafes, restaurants, or small shops to refuel and hydrate. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between villages.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route partially overlaps with other paths. You will encounter sections of the Sierras Riojanas and the Camino verde del Alhama, among others, for short stretches.

waypoints.name: Views of the Castillo de Magaña
